skillName: Use formal methods for subtraction of integers
misconceptionSummary: Students think borrowing permanently reduces the number — they confuse the working notation with the value.

visuals.ruleText: Borrowing is regrouping: 1 ten becomes 10 ones — the total value is unchanged
visuals.subText: You are not reducing the number; you are exchanging one unit for ten smaller units

steps.narration: Borrowing does not reduce the value of the number. Trading one ten for ten ones gives you more ones to work with while the total stays exactly the same — like exchanging a ten-pound note for ten one-pound coins.

lines.text: Exchange 1 ten for 10 ones: tens 5→4, ones 2→12

steps.narration: When we write four in the tens after borrowing, we record that one ten has been moved — exchanged for ten ones. The reduction in the tens column and the increase in the ones column are two sides of the same exchange.

lines.text: Use this to verify any column subtraction

steps.narration: Check every subtraction by adding the answer back to the number subtracted. Forty-five plus seven is fifty-two — that confirms the answer is correct. Inverse operations are a powerful checking tool.

visuals.label: Borrow = regroup 1 ten as 10 ones — total value unchanged

steps.narration: Forty-five. Borrowing is a fair exchange — one ten for ten ones — not a reduction in value.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: thinking borrowing 'uses up' part of the number. Borrowing is an exchange — 1 ten becomes 10 ones. The total value before and after the exchange is identical.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Borrowing is like breaking a ten-pound note into coins. You have the same amount — just in smaller units. The total value never changes.
